content.text: Roadmap Dependency Sequencing and Horizon Planning

A Claude adoption roadmap is not a flat list of initiatives — it is a network of interdependent capabilities where some items are prerequisites for others. Attempting to deploy use cases before governance exists, or expanding to new business units before the service catalog is operational, produces predictable failures. Dependency sequencing makes those prerequisites explicit so the roadmap reflects how capabilities actually build on each other.

Horizon planning complements dependency sequencing by organizing the roadmap into time-based views (Now, Next, Later) that allow stakeholders to understand current priorities without losing sight of the long-term trajectory.
Why Dependencies Matter in Claude Roadmaps

Claude roadmap items span multiple capability categories — technical, organizational, governance, and enablement — and these categories are tightly coupled. A common failure mode is treating these as independent work streams that can proceed in parallel without coordination:
Deploying use cases before governance is ready:

teams build Claude integrations but discover they have no clear data handling policy, no risk assessment process, and no way to approve production deployments. Deployments stall or proceed with unmanaged risk.
Building a service catalog before the intake process exists:
the catalog is defined but teams submit requests via email anyway because no one built the intake form or ticketing integration.
Activating BU champions before training exists:
champions are named but have no enablement resources to share with their teams. They lose credibility quickly.
Scaling use cases before evaluation frameworks exist:
dozens of Claude integrations run in production with no standard for measuring whether outputs are actually good. Quality problems compound invisibly.
In Claude roadmaps, sequencing errors are often invisible until something breaks. A governance framework built late feels like a bureaucratic add-on; built first, it feels like infrastructure.

attrs.alt: Claude roadmap dependency graph — foundation to strategic layers

content.text: The Four Dependency Layers
Claude roadmap items cluster into four layers, each dependent on the layer below:
Layer 1: Foundation (Must Come First)
Foundation items are prerequisites for almost everything else. They cannot be parallelized with upper layers — they must be substantially complete before enablement and delivery work can proceed.
Legal and security framework:
acceptable use policy, data classification rules, API security controls. Without this, every use case is operating in a policy vacuum.
API access and data controls:
procurement, authentication, environment configuration, network security. Teams cannot build or test without API access.
Risk assessment rubric v1:
the criteria for evaluating whether a use case is low, medium, or high risk. Without this, the CoE cannot consistently triage requests.
CoE team assembled:
at minimum, CoE Lead and one AI Engineer. Without accountable humans, the program exists on paper only.
Foundation work typically takes 6–10 weeks in an organization that prioritizes it. Legal review of the acceptable use policy and procurement of API access are the most common bottlenecks.
Layer 2: Enablement (Builds on Foundation)

Enablement items prepare the organization to use Claude effectively. They depend on foundation being complete (you can't train teams on a tool they don't have access to) and create the conditions for delivery to succeed.
Prompt engineering training:

teams need baseline skills before they can contribute to pilot use cases or evaluate Claude outputs. Training delivery requires API access (for live practice) and governance clarity (so trainers can answer 'can we use Claude for X?' questions accurately.
Service catalog and intake process:
the operating mechanism for CoE demand management. Depends on CoE team being assembled and the risk rubric existing (intake forms ask for risk tier).
BU champion network:
champions cannot be effective without access to training materials, the service catalog, and CoE support. Designating champions before these exist creates false starts.
Prompt library v1:
curated, validated prompts from pilot use cases. The library depends on pilots existing — it cannot be pre-built in the abstract.
Layer 3: Delivery (Builds on Enablement)
Delivery items are the actual Claude use cases and the systems that support them at scale:
Pilot use cases (2–5):
the first production deployments. Depend on API access, governance framework, and at least one AI Engineer to review integrations.
Production scale (10+ use cases):
expanding beyond pilots. Depends on service catalog (for intake management), champions (for BU-level coordination), and the prompt library (to avoid reinvention).
Federated BU delivery:
BUs owning use case delivery autonomously. Depends on champion network being active and evaluation standards existing so quality can be assessed without central review.
Self-service portal:
advanced intake automation. Depends on service catalog being stable and ticketing integration complete — the portal automates a process that must first be manually validated.
Layer 4: Strategic (Builds on Delivery at Scale)
Strategic items are only meaningful once the program has meaningful scale and data:
Steering committee and OKRs:
executive reviews of Claude program performance. Depend on monthly operations reporting existing and metrics being collected.
Innovation pipeline:
evaluating new Claude capabilities as they are released. Depends on having production use cases to apply new capabilities against — innovation in the abstract is low value.
Competitive advantage positioning:
presenting Claude as a strategic differentiator externally. Depends on having documented, finance-reviewed ROI cases and a track record of successful delivery.
Mapping Dependencies: A Practical Format
Dependencies can be documented at three levels of formality depending on organizational context:
Dependency Table (Lightweight)
Roadmap Item
Depends On
Blocks
Prompt engineering training
API access; Legal framework v1
Pilot use cases; BU champion effectiveness
Service catalog launch
CoE team assembled; Risk rubric v1
Structured intake; Champion delegation
Pilot use case #1 in production
API access; Legal framework; CoE AI Engineer
Prompt library; Production scale
BU champion designation
Service catalog; Training materials
Federated delivery; BU autonomous use cases
ROI documentation
Pilot in production; Finance partnership
Phase 2 funding case; Steering committee credibility
Federated delivery model
Champions active; Evaluation standards; Tier 2 decision rights
Self-service; Scale beyond 20 use cases
Critical Path Analysis
The critical path is the sequence of dependencies that determines the minimum time to reach a milestone. For most Claude programs, the critical path to Phase 1 completion runs:

# Critical path to first production use case

1. Legal/security framework (4–6 weeks)
   ├── Legal review of acceptable use policy: 2–3 weeks
   └── Security review of API integration patterns: 2–3 weeks

2. API access procurement (overlaps with step 1: 2–4 weeks)
   ├── Vendor contract execution: 1–2 weeks
   └── Security controls configured: 1–2 weeks

3. CoE team assembled (can overlap with steps 1–2: 3–6 weeks)
   ├── CoE Lead identified and onboarded: 2–4 weeks
   └── AI Engineer hired/assigned: 3–6 weeks (often the critical constraint)

4. Risk rubric v1 complete (1–2 weeks, after legal framework)

5. Pilot use case design and build (4–6 weeks, after all above)
   ├── Use case selection and scoping: 1 week
   ├── Prompt design and testing: 2–3 weeks
   └── Integration build and security review: 1–2 weeks

Minimum time to first production use case:
  Fully parallel execution: ~10 weeks
  Sequential execution: ~18 weeks
  Typical real-world: ~12–14 weeks (partial parallelism, some delays)
Horizon Planning
Horizon planning divides the roadmap into three time-bounded views that give different stakeholders what they need:
Horizon 1: Now (0–3 months)
Items in active execution. This horizon should have:
Clear ownership for every item (specific person or team)
Defined done criteria — what does completion actually look like?
Weekly tracking against milestones
All dependencies for Horizon 1 items already resolved or explicitly in progress
Horizon 1 typically contains 4–8 items. More than 10 items in active execution usually signals the team is over-committed and key items will stall.
Horizon 2: Next (3–9 months)
Items planned for the near future. This horizon should have:
Rough estimates and target quarters
Dependency analysis — which Horizon 1 items must complete before Horizon 2 can start?
Resource pre-allocation — which team members will be assigned to Horizon 2 items?
Risk identification — what could prevent Horizon 2 items from starting on time?
Horizon 2 planning is reviewed monthly and items are refined as they approach the Horizon 1 threshold.
Horizon 3: Later (9–24 months)
Strategic intentions that are not yet fully planned. This horizon should have:
Directional descriptions, not detailed plans
Strategic goals and hypotheses about value
Expected dependencies on Horizon 2 completion
No detailed resource allocation — this is subject to change

# Example horizon plan (Month 6 snapshot)

horizon_1_now:  # Months 6-9
  - item: Service catalog v1 launch
    owner: CoE Lead
    done_when: Intake form live; ticketing integrated; 5 services published with SLAs
    depends_on: [CoE team assembled, Risk rubric v1]
    status: in_progress

  - item: BU champion designation — Finance and Eng
    owner: CoE Lead + Finance/Eng leadership
    done_when: Champions named, chartered, 20% time allocation confirmed
    depends_on: [Training materials v1, Service catalog]
    status: in_progress

  - item: Prompt engineering training cohort 1
    owner: Enablement Specialist
    done_when: 25+ participants across 2 BUs; satisfaction >= 4/5
    depends_on: [API access, Legal framework]
    status: complete

horizon_2_next:  # Months 9-15
  - item: 10 production use cases milestone
    estimate: Q4 2025
    depends_on: [champion network active, service catalog stable]

  - item: ROI documentation — 2 use cases
    estimate: Q4 2025
    depends_on: [pilots in production >= 60 days, Finance partnership]

  - item: Self-service intake portal
    estimate: Q1 2026
    depends_on: [service catalog stable, ticketing system mature]

horizon_3_later:  # Months 15-24+
  - item: Federated operating model
    direction: BUs operate autonomously; CoE is standards and support
    depends_on: [10+ prod use cases, champions active, evaluation standards]

  - item: Innovation pipeline
    direction: Structured process for evaluating new Claude capabilities
    depends_on: [production scale, steering committee established]
Managing Dependency Conflicts
Dependency conflicts arise when multiple Horizon 1 items compete for the same scarce resource (usually CoE team time or legal/security bandwidth). Resolution approaches:
Prioritize by critical path impact:
which items, if delayed, delay the most downstream work? These get priority for scarce resources.
Time-box dependencies:
if legal review is a bottleneck, define a maximum wait time (e.g., 3 weeks), then escalate to the executive sponsor if not resolved. Unbounded waits turn into indefinite delays.
Pre-read parallelism:

some foundation items can be partially parallelized. Legal framework and API access procurement can both start on Day 1 even though API access formally depends on the legal framework — the legal review and procurement processes are parallel activities.
Explicit blockers:

in milestone tracking, maintain a 'blocking issues' field for any item that cannot progress due to an unresolved dependency. Blockers surfaced explicitly are resolved faster than blockers buried in status updates.
Dependency Sequencing for Specific Use Case Types
Different categories of Claude use cases have different prerequisite stacks. Understanding these prevents teams from underestimating what it takes to deploy:
Use Case Type
Required Prerequisites
Common Missing Dependency
Internal productivity (summarization, drafting)
API access, basic prompt training, acceptable use policy
Policy clarity on what internal data can be submitted
Customer-facing output
Full governance review, human-in-the-loop process, CSAT measurement
Human review workflow; output quality evaluation standard
RAG / document Q&A system
API access, vector database, data ingestion pipeline, chunking strategy
Data pipeline infrastructure often underestimated
Agentic / tool-use system
Tier 4 governance approval, human-in-the-loop, incident response plan, blast radius analysis
Accountable human for autonomous actions often undefined
Multi-tenant SaaS feature (Claude in product)
API rate limits, per-customer cost tracking, output moderation, enterprise terms
Per-customer cost attribution is frequently missed
Communicating the Roadmap to Stakeholders

Different stakeholders need different roadmap views. Presenting the same dependency-sequenced roadmap to every audience creates confusion — executives want strategy, engineers want implementation detail, and BU leaders want to know when something will be available for their team.
Executive View: Strategic Horizon Roadmap
Executives need to see the big picture — what is the Claude program committing to, by when, and what does success look like at each stage. The executive roadmap should:
Show three to four major milestones per phase, not every sub-task
Highlight business outcomes (first production use case, first ROI case, federated model activated) rather than technical deliverables
Include approximate dates expressed as quarters, not specific days — this is appropriate given the inherent uncertainty of a 24-month program
Show the dependency between phases explicitly: 'Phase 2 begins after Phase 1 foundation is complete'

# Executive Roadmap (one-page view)

Q1–Q2 2025: Foundation
  Goal: Claude program operational with first production use case
  Key milestones: Legal framework, API access, CoE team, 1 use case in prod

Q3–Q4 2025: Growth
  Goal: 3+ BUs using Claude; service catalog operational
  Key milestones: Service catalog, champion network, 10 use cases, first ROI case

Q1–Q2 2026: Scale
  Goal: Federated delivery; organization-wide Claude literacy
  Key milestones: Federated model, self-service, 20+ use cases, steering committee

Q3 2026+: Optimize
  Goal: Embedded AI engineers; Claude as competitive differentiator
  Key milestones: Platform model, innovation pipeline, executive showcase
CoE Team View: Dependency-Sequenced Work Plan
The CoE team needs the full dependency graph with ownership, status, and blockers. This is the operational roadmap — updated weekly, used in standup, and reviewed monthly in milestone reviews.
Every item has an owner (specific name, not just a role)
Every item has a dependency list (what must be done first)
Every item has a current status and a blocker field
Items are sorted by dependency order, not alphabetically or by date
The work plan is maintained in the CoE's project management tool (Jira, Linear, Notion, etc.)
BU Stakeholder View: Service Roadmap
Business unit leaders want to know: when will Claude be available for my team, and what can they do with it? The BU-facing roadmap should show:
When the service catalog will be available and how to request services
Training schedule — when are the next prompt engineering workshops?
Expected timeline to first production use case for their BU (gated by champion designation and use case readiness)
New capabilities coming in the next 6 months that their BU should consider for use case pipeline
A roadmap that no one can read is just a document. Design each view for its audience — the same underlying dependency data, presented in the format each stakeholder can act on.
Replanning When Dependencies Break

Even well-planned roadmaps break. Legal review takes twice as long as expected; a key engineer leaves; a security incident forces a policy redesign. When dependencies break, replan deliberately rather than absorbing the delay silently:
Surface the impact immediately:
if the legal framework is 3 weeks late, communicate immediately that the pilot use case timeline is affected. Late disclosure of delays erodes trust more than the delays themselves.
Resequence, don't just shift dates:

when a dependency slips, don't just push everything downstream by the same number of weeks. Ask: can any downstream items begin earlier with partial dependency completion? Are there parallel paths that were previously de-prioritized?
Document the replan decision:
log the reason for the dependency break, the options considered, and the decision made. This creates institutional memory and reduces the chance of the same mistake in future phases.
Communicate to affected stakeholders:

BU leaders who were expecting a service catalog in April need to know if it's now June — especially if they've committed to their teams that training or use case work would begin based on the original date.
Summary
Dependency sequencing and horizon planning transform a Claude wishlist into an executable roadmap. They prevent the predictable failures that come from parallelizing inherently sequential work.
Organize roadmap items into four dependency layers: Foundation → Enablement → Delivery → Strategic
Foundation (legal framework, API access, risk rubric, CoE team) must be substantially complete before enablement and delivery can succeed
Map explicit dependencies for every roadmap item: what must exist before this can start, and what does this unblock?
The critical path to first production use case is typically 10–14 weeks; AI Engineer hiring and legal review are the most common bottlenecks

Use three-horizon planning: Horizon 1 (Now, 0–3 months) with ownership and weekly tracking; Horizon 2 (Next, 3–9 months) with dependencies and resource pre-allocation; Horizon 3 (Later, 9–24 months) with directional intent
Manage dependency conflicts by prioritizing critical path items, time-boxing dependency waits, and surfacing blockers explicitly in milestone reviews
Different use case types have different prerequisite stacks — agentic and customer-facing use cases require governance prerequisites that internal productivity use cases do not
